nativeRange.habitat: Coral reef lagoons and outer reef slopes; found on live rock, rubble, and sandy patches with established microfauna.

decorPreferences: mature live rock with biofilm and microfauna
open sand and rubble patches for grazing

diet.notes: Grazes on biofilm, detritus, microalgae, and sponge growth across live rock. In well-established tanks, generally sustains itself on microfauna and aufwuchs; supplemental offerings of small pieces of shrimp, mussel, or sinking pellets may be required in newer or oligotrophic systems.

avoidWith: Harlequin Shrimp (Hymenocera picta) — obligate seastar predator

summary: The Indian Fromia Sea Star is a vividly mottled brick-red to orange-red Indo-Pacific sea star, generally considered reef-safe, that grazes biofilm and detritus and requires a well-matured tank with stable water chemistry.
careNotes: The Indian Fromia Sea Star (Fromia indica) is a small Indo-Pacific echinoderm with a brick-red to orange-red body marked with a fine mesh of darker lines and pale mottling. It is generally considered reef-safe and is commonly kept alongside LPS, SPS, and soft corals, where it grazes biofilm and detritus rather than tissue. Like most Fromia, it has been reported to be sensitive to sudden shifts in salinity, pH, and dissolved oxygen — slow drip acclimation over 1–2 hours is generally advised, and the animal should never be exposed to air during transfer. It tends to do best in a well-matured aquarium (commonly recommended at least 6 months established) with ample live rock that has accumulated microfauna and aufwuchs to graze. Copper-based medications are not tolerated and should never enter the system. Care broadly parallels the closely related Fromia milleporella.
